date: 2026-06-11
video_id: 3v3RXxEGbQY
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3v3RXxEGbQY
title: The Macro Minute: Should the Fed look through current accelerating above-target inflation?

Happy Thursday out there at 13:42, it's your skipper here Darius Dale to present our macro minute for Thursday, June 11th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's Lead Off Morning Note, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, should the Fed look through the current bout of accelerating above-target inflation? The short answer is no. If it does, the stock market bubble will continue to inflate. Three orthogonal vectors are combining right now to signal to members of the FOMC that they must tighten to combat the build-up of core inflationary pressure in the US economy. Number one, the massive aggregate demand shock from AI CapEx is combining with procyclical fiscal stimulus from Abba. Number two, the sharp slowdowns in labor supply and productivity growth suggest the risk of the economy overheating from a NAIRU and output gap perspective is high. And number three, the monetary drivers of inflation are nearing dangerous tipping points that may begin to spiral if the Fed commits to easy money. Most importantly, none of these vectors has anything to do with the US-Israel Iran conflict, which means the risk of the Fed targeting the wealth effect channel to rein in price pressures this summer is much higher than currently assumed by the median market participant. Refer to our June 6th Around the Horn webcast titled Is It Time to Party Like It's 1998? for more details. In terms of analytical nuance, the May PPI data supported the consensus outlook for peaking inflation. The May federal budget statement data did not. The May headline PPI, core PPI, core PPI ex-trade services, goods PPI, and services PPI data strongly supported our staking inflation theme, as did the May median CPI and trim mean CPI data, which are both in strong positive impulses at levels that are wickedly inconsistent with the Fed's 2% inflation target. Core PPI has been a leading indicator of core CPI and core PCE in recent inflation cycles. The structurally elevated peaks and troughs in core PPI perfectly presage structurally elevated peaks and troughs in core CPI and core PCE. The cresting of core PPI at 4.9% year-over-year in May, may be a harbinger of future good news on the inflation targeting front. The ongoing acceleration in headline PPI, which has been an equally prescient leading indicator for core PCE in recent inflation cycles, tells a different story. Specifically, the acceleration to a well-above-trend rate of 6.5% calls into question how much of the negative energy supply shock and positive AI demand shock will contribute to accelerating inflation in the core categories the Fed cares most about in the subsequent months. There is a credible case to be made that the Fed should look through the lagged impact of the negative energy supply shock upon inflation. There is no credible case to be made that the Fed should look through well-above-trend year-over-year rate growth rates of federal deficit spending, Fed public debt monetization, and bank credit growth, especially with the lagged impact of 175 basis points of rate cuts that are currently applying downward pressure on aggregate demand. There's also no credible case to be made that the Fed should look through the strong positive impulses in the growth rates of base money, narrow money, and broad money, especially not amid cyclically and structurally elevated levels of M1 and M2 money velocity. The only saving grace here is that both M1 and M2 are only growing at trend levels. Accelerations beyond these thresholds will shut down the look-through narrative. And finally, in the Eurozone, the ECB's decision to hike rates is consistent with the consensus outlook for sticky, well-above-target inflation. The ECB raised its 2026 and 2027 headline CPI forecasts to above-target rates, raised its 2026, 2027, and 2028 core CPI forecasts to above-target rates, and reduced its 2026 and 2027 real GDP forecasts to below-trend rates. Hiking rates into anticipated stagflation signals the ECB is a central bank that is actually committed to price stability. How will How will the Kevin Warsh Fed respond to the signal? We shall find out. Is Kevin Warsh a patsy, or is he uh, you know and then just been paying lip service to this whole price stability thing and and protecting the the families on the low end of the K, or or is he serious about you know his his his his hawkish you know his legacy of hawkish views and and and and and as a defender of sound money and sound orthodox you know sound economics and and and and price stability. We'll find out. We don't know. These are that's a big known unknown right now. What is true underlying reaction function is separate and apart from the the the you know the the the the process he had to undergo to get the job and and also we we don't really know another known unknown is we don't know how the committee's going to respond to him considering the fact that he's was been hyper critical of them for years. So, we're going to find out next Wednesday. You know, we're not going to find out everything next Wednesday but we're going to get the first signals next Wednesday in markets. You know, if the markets aren't going to like Kevin Warsh that is an actual hawk in dove's clothing. So, transition to our 42 macro dashboard here as always we wrap up with a question from our community. This one's titled 42 macro money managers. If I were looking to one we're looking to have their money managed professionally instead of DIY do it yourself, are there any recommendations for trusted 42 macro money managers who subscribe to the 42 macro thesis and structure manage client portfolios to react based on 42 macro signals. Curious to have anyone experience with this or have used any such service. So, the answer is resounding yes. We do have a partnership that we're finalizing legal docs on that so I'm not going to front run the announcement there but yes, we do have a money manager that we are partnered with our partnering with that is a wonderful track record, wonderful human being, great teams and more most importantly thinks about risk management the way we do and actually manages hundreds of millions of dollars of client assets with our signals. So, if you want to get in touch with that money manager go to 42macro.com/contact. You can just go to that from our our homepage here. Click on this hamburger menu at the top left and then go to contact and then then shoot shoot our uh, our team an email regarding this opportunity. So, uh so very excited about that partnership. You know, it's going to do some some great things for the for the investors in our community and the families in our communities. They have one of the world's best financial planners on their on their roster. So, I'm excited about that personally as well as for our business. So, we're wrapping up there. Jerry Cell here presenting our macro minute for Thursday, June 11th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
